messages.content:
reword this: Response 1

Who suffers loss if Muhammad was wrong? Sura 34:50 commands Muhammad to say, “If I go astray, I go astray only to my own loss,” which is a severe factual error in the Qur’an as well as contradicting the teaching of the Qur’an in a number of other verses
Reply:

The verses is

Say, “If I should err, I would only err against myself. But if I am guided, it is by what my Lord reveals to me. Indeed, He is Hearing and near..(34;50)

First of all,the verse starts with “IF’‘.So It is not a definite condition at all.

Secondly if Muhammad sallallahu alaihi wa aalihi wasallam would be wrong,then he could not have continued.

[It is] a revelation from the Lord of the worlds.

And if Muhammad had made up about Us some [false] sayings,

We would have seized him by the right hand

Then We would have cut from him the aorta

And there is no one of you who could prevent [Us] from him.(69;43-47)

.

But as my Holy Prophet Muhammad s.a.w.w had continued to preach the revealtion of Allah,it means that infact,He was totally and absolutely right.He was guided by Allah’s revealation.The second condition mentioned in the verse was true for Prophet Muhammad s.a.w.w.

Another verse form Holy Quran,

So rely upon Allah ; indeed, you are upon the clear truth..naml 79

Note that there is no IF or but in the verse mentioned above,but there is a word INDEED so it is the true condition that Prophet Muhammad s.a.w.w. was on clear truth,guided by Allah’s revelation.

Revised Response:

Concerning the liability in the event of Muhammad's error, Surah 34:50 states that Muhammad is instructed to proclaim, “If I err, I err only to my detriment.” This assertion appears to represent a significant misstatement within the Qur’an, while also seemingly conflicting with the Qur'an's teachings elsewhere.

Regarding the passage,

It reads, “Say, ‘If I err, my error impacts only myself. Yet if I am rightly guided, it is due to my Lord's revelations. Truly, He is All-Hearing, All-Near.’ (34:50)”

Initially, we must recognize that the verse opens with “IF,” indicating a hypothetical scenario rather than an absolute one.

Moreover, had Muhammad (peace be upon him and his family) been mistaken, his prophetic mission would not have endured.

The text proclaims:

This message is surely a revelation from the Lord of all worlds.

Had Muhammad fabricated any falsehoods about Us,

We would have surely seized him by his right hand,

And then severed his lifeline,

And not one among you could have defended him. (69:43-47)

Given that our beloved Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him and his family) continued to disseminate Allah's revelations, it signifies that in reality, he was entirely and unequivocally correct. He was guided by the revelation of Allah. The latter scenario depicted in the initial verse reflects Prophet Muhammad’s (peace be upon him and his family) true state.

Another verse from the Holy Qur'an affirms:

So, place your trust in Allah; indeed, you stand upon the plain truth. (An-Naml 79)

It's important to note that unlike the previous verse with ‘IF,’ this verse utilizes the term ‘INDEAD,’ reinforcing the indisputable truth that Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him and his family) was on the clear truth, guided by Allah’s revelation.

reword this: Critic:
How did the angels know about humans?
Reply:
If there is nothing about it in Quran,it is NEVER any contradiction.

Critic:

Teaching the names only to Adam a.s,not to angels?
Reply:
It was not to challenge them,but to show about the special Quality granted by Allah to the humans,which was not granted to angels.

Critic:

And how does the naming of things justify the creation of man despite all the evil and violence he will commit?
Reply:
It clearly tells about the gift of Knowledge and the gift of brain (reasoning,memory,logic,intelligence),in superiority of angels..and so they were sent to earth for trial…so that their good or bad deeds would be judged.

Critic:Bowing before man…..
Reply:
Just a sign of honor.Not worship,even christians bow /kneel down before popes and I m sure that it would not be worship according to you.

Critic:

Fourth, Iblis or Satan is blamed for not worshiping Adam even though the command was given to angels
Reply:
Angels ,malaaikah.
It is a plural word,just see the meanings of singular.
It is angel,cadre and personnel.

Iblees was the only one who denied to obey.

Critic:
Iblees,angel or jinn

Reply:
Clearly mentioned in quran that Iblees was a jinn.
“Behold! We said to the angels, “Bow down to Adam.” they bowed down except Iblis He was one of the Jinns.”
[Al-Qur’an 18:50]

Critic:
The Quran is totally silent on the creation of angel
Reply:
Not a contradiction at all.

Critic:
why Adam was in heaven when he was created to dwell on the earth as Allah’s vice-regent. He wasn’t created to dwell in heaven___To mention these things before letting Him and His wife to go to earth
Reply:
1.To mention Adam a.s and Hawwa a.s that Iblees and humans are enemies.
2.To mention that who will Follow Allah’s guidance,would not suffer.
3.To mention that Allah is forgiving for those who repent.
But Satan caused them to slip out of it and removed them from that [condition] in which they had been. And We said, “Go down, [all of you], as enemies to one another, and you will have upon the earth a place of settlement and provision for a time .Baqarah 36
Then Adam received from his Lord [some] words, and He accepted his repentance. Indeed, it is He who is the Accepting of repentance, the Merciful..Baqarah 37
We said, “Go down from it, all of you. And when guidance comes to you from Me, whoever follows My guidance – there will be no fear concerning them, nor will they grieve….Baqarah 38

Critic:

This therefore shows that the descent wasn’t solely in rank or position. The descent was a literal one, being cast out of a higher plane or realm (the heavenly Garden) to a lower one..

15:28-44,. 38:71-85
Reply:
Anyone of you can check it that nothing about the descent of Prophet Adam a.s. is discussed in any of these 2 surahs.
Secondly the word used in both surahs for Iblees to be expelled is “fakh’ruj” ,and the root word is “khrj” which means “out”
I agree that the descent was literal,but not because of these references,but the word “Ihbit” _to go down/slip down (in surah baqarah)
Heavens are at a higher place than the world.AND WORLD IS NOT PARADISE__HEAVENS /PARADISE and WORLD are two different places.
But Satan caused them to slip out of it and removed them from that [condition] in which they had been. And We said, “Go down, [all of you], as enemies to one another, and you will have upon the earth a place of settlement and provision for a time…baqarah 36

Critic:

Sixth, Adam’s sin clearly impacted all future generations of mankind since in both 2:36 and 38 the plural (more than two) is used, as opposed to the dual.
Reply:
Prophet Adam a.s,His wife,and Iblees,these 3 are mentioned ,and for three, plural is to be used.
It was the matter of predestination for which Adam a.s. could NOT be blamed.

Narrated Abu Huraira:
The Prophet said, “Adam and Moses argued with each other. Moses said to Adam. ‘O Adam! You are our father WHO DISAPPOINTED US AND TURNED US OUT OF PARADISE.’ Then Adam said to him, ‘O Moses! Allah favored you with His talk (talked to you directly) and He wrote (the Torah) for you with His Own Hand. Do you blame me for action which Allah had written in my fate forty years before my creation?‘ So Adam confuted Moses, Adam confuted Moses,” the Prophet added, repeating the Statement three times. (Sahih Al-Bukhari, Volume 8, Book 77, Number 611)

According to Quran,it was decided before the creation of Adam a.s. that He would be the viceroy of Allah on the Earth.

And [mention, O Muhammad], when your Lord said to the angels, “Indeed, I will make upon the earth a successive authority.” They said, “Will You place upon it one who causes corruption therein and sheds blood, while we declare Your praise and sanctify You?” Allah said, “Indeed, I know that which you do not know….Baqarah 30..
Prophet Adam a.s. was not created then ,and it was already decided that He would dwell on earth.
The dialogue between Prophet Musa a.s. and Prophet Adam a.s also tells that It was personal opinion of Prophet Musa a.s to accuse Prophet Adam a.s.Not the fact.Also note that it is mentioned that Prophet Adam a.s confuted Prophet Musa a.s.

There is no such concept in Islam that Adam a.s caused the offspring to be expelled from Heavens

The query regarding how angels could have knowledge of humans is addressed with the assertion that absence of explicit explanation in the Qur'an does not equate to contradiction.

In discussing why only Adam (peace be upon him) was taught names and not angels, the reply emphasizes that the purpose was not to challenge but to demonstrate the unique capacity endowed upon humanity by Allah, a capacity not given to angels.

The act of teaching Adam the names serves to underscore the bestowed gift of knowledge, reason, memory, logic, and intelligence upon humans, which surpasses that of angels. This knowledge and intelligence are reasons humans were placed on Earth for a trial to determine their deeds.

The critic's mention of angels bowing before humanity is responded to by stating that the gesture was an honorific one, comparable to how some Christians may bow or kneel before religious leaders, which is not considered worship.

Regarding the criticism of Satan being reprimanded for not bowing to Adam, the reply clarifies that "angels" is a plural term and Iblis was the sole figure who refused obedience.

Satan's nature is discussed, with the Qur'an explicitly identifying Iblis as a jinn, not an angel, citing Quran 18:50.

When questioned about the silence of the Qur'an on the creation of angels, the reply remains firm that this does not represent a contradiction.

In response to the issue of Adam existing in heaven, it is explained that the purpose was multifaceted: to alert Adam and Eve of their enemy Iblis, to convey that those who follow divine guidance will not suffer, and to proclaim Allah's forgiving nature for those who repent. It is affirmed that Adam's descent to Earth was a literal relocation, evidenced by the Quranic term "Ihbit," which means to descend or go down.

Lastly, addressing the claim that Adam's sin affected all future generations, the response points out that Quranic plural references can also include Adam, Eve, and Iblis, without assigning blame to Adam for predestined actions. The debate between Prophets Moses and Adam is cited to illustrate that it was considered unjust to blame Adam for his actions, which were predestined. The Qur'an had already proclaimed the intention to establish a steward on Earth before Adam was created, showing that his stay on Earth was preordained and not a punishment.

Here's the reformatted text:

Critic: "How did the angels know about humans?"

Reply: The absence of specific information in the Quran does not inherently indicate a contradiction.

Critic: "Why was the knowledge of names taught only to Adam and not to angels?"

Reply: The intent was to highlight the unique ability granted to humans by Allah, an ability that angels do not possess.

Critic: "How can the naming of things justify the creation of man, despite the potential for evil and violence?"

Reply: The act symbolizes the endowment of knowledge and cognitive faculties—reasoning, memory, logic, intelligence—proving humanity's superiority over angels and setting the stage for the earthly trial of deeds.

Critic: "Why the command for angels to bow before man?"

Reply: This was a gesture of respect, not of worship, akin to how Christians may show reverence to religious figures without it being considered worship.

Critic: "Why is Iblis, or Satan, blamed for not worshiping Adam when the order was for angels?"

Reply: "Angels" is a plural term; the singular denotes an individual angel, cadre, or member. Iblis alone chose to defy the command.

Critic: "Is Iblis an angel or a jinn?"

Reply: The Quran specifies that Iblis was a jinn, as stated in [Al-Quran 18:50], where all angels except for Iblis, a jinn, obeyed the command to bow to Adam.

Critic: "Why is there no account of the creation of angels in the Quran?"

Reply: This does not represent a contradiction.

Critic: "Why was Adam in heaven initially if he was meant to dwell on Earth?"

Reply: Three reasons are provided:

1. To reveal that Iblis and humans are adversaries.
2. To affirm that followers of Allah's guidance will not endure hardship.
3. To demonstrate Allah's readiness to forgive those who repent.

Critic: "The descent from the Garden suggests that it was a literal fall to a lower realm, not just in rank."

Reply: Surahs 15:28-44 and 38:71-85 do not describe Adam's descent. The word "fakhruj" means "out," implying expulsion, and "Ihbit" from Surah Al-Baqarah indicates a literal descent from a higher place (heaven) to a lower one (earth).

Critic: "Adam's sin seems to have impacted all his progeny, as the plural form is used."

Reply: The plural is used because it references Adam, his wife, and Iblis. Adam is not blamed for a predestined event. The dialogue between Prophets Moses and Adam, as reported by Abu Huraira, shows Adam's actions were part of his fate, acknowledged before his creation. Moreover, the Quran reveals that it was predetermined for Adam to be a caretaker on Earth, and this dialogue suggests that Moses' accusation was a personal stance, not established fact.

In Islam, there is no concept of Adam's actions causing his descendants to be banished from heaven.

reword this: Allah, Adam, and the Angels. There are a great number of problems and
inconsistencies between the several accounts of Adam’s creation, Allah’s command
to prostrate before Adam, Satans refusal, etc.Response

The narrative encompassing Allah, Adam, and the angels indeed raises several points of discussion and perceived discrepancies among the various recountings of Adam's creation, the divine edict to prostrate to Adam, and Satan's subsequent defiance.

reformat this: Who was the First Muslim?

Response:

Not a contradiction at all if you know the meanings of “muslim”.

Muslim is the one who Submits His will before Allah.

If in a group of three people,anyone says that I have submitted my will before Allah i.e I am going to deny my will,I am just going to do whatever I have been ordered by Allah.It means that the person is the first of all those three to be a Muslim.Similarly,in a different group,if anyone else says the same,he would also be considered as first Muslim among those people..i.e in that group.

Understanding the Concept of the First Muslim:

The perceived contradiction concerning who the first Muslim was dissolves when one comprehends the definition of 'Muslim'. A Muslim is someone who surrenders their will to Allah.

For example, in a group of three individuals, if one person declares they have surrendered their will to Allah—meaning they reject personal desires to faithfully follow Allah's commands—this person is regarded as the first Muslim within that particular trio. Likewise, in a different group, anyone making a similar declaration would be seen as the first Muslim among that assembly. Thus, the term 'first Muslim' can be contextually applied to various individuals in separate groups or at different times.

reformat this: 

To summarize the confusion and contradictions:
Did Muhammad see Allah or did he see Gabriel?
Reply:
_By the Star when it plunges, your comrade is not astray, neither errs, nor speaks he out of caprice. This is naught but a revelation revealed, taught HIM by ONE terrible in power, very strong; HE STOOD POISED, being on the higher horizon, THEN DREW NEAR and suspended hung, two bows’-length away, or nearer, then REVEALED TO HIS SERVANT that HE REVEALED. His heart lies not of what he saw; what, will you dispute with him what he sees? Indeed, he SAW HIM ANOTHER TIME by the Lote-Tree of the Boundary nigh which is the Garden of the Refuge, when there covered the Lote-Tree that which covered; his eye swerved not; nor swept astray. Indeed, he saw one of the greatest signs of his Lord. S. 53:1-18
Quran simply says that The Prophet Muhammad s.a.w.w. had seen one of the Greatest signs of Allah,He had seen Angel Jibraeel.Allah can NOT be seen.(read the hadees given below)
Critic:Were Ibn Abbas and the majority Or were Aisha, Ibn Masud, Abu Hurayra, and Abu Dharr correct that it wasn’t his lord that he saw, but Gabriel?
Reply:
The collection of ahadees of Sahih Bukhari and Sahih Muslim are most authentic.
 
It is narrated on the authority of Masruq that he said: I was resting at (the house of) ‘A’isha that she said: O Abu ‘A’isha (kunya of Masruq), there are three things, and he who affirmed even one of them fabricated the greatest lie against Allah. I asked what they were. She said: He who presumed that Muhammad (may peace be upon him) saw his Lord (with his ocular vision) fabricated the greatest lie against Allah. I was reclining but then sat up and said: Mother of the Faithful, wait a bit and do not be in a haste. Has not Allah (Mighty and Majestic) said: “And truly he saw him on the clear horizon” (al-Qur’an, lxxxi. 23) and “he saw Him in another descent” (al-Qur’an, liii. 13)? She said: I am the first of this Ummah who asked the Messenger of Allah (may peace be upon him) about it, and he said: Verily he is Gabriel. I have never seen him in his original form in which he was created except on those two occasions (to which these verses refer); I saw him descending from the heaven and filling (the space) from the sky to the earth with the greatness of his bodily structure. She said: Have you not heard Allah saying: “Eyes comprehend Him not, but He comprehends (all) vision. And He is Subtle, and All-Aware” (al-Qur’an, vi. 103)? (She, i. e. ‘A’isha, further said): Have you not heard that, verily, Allah says: “And it is not vouchsafed to a human being that Allah should speak unto him otherwise than by revelation, or from behind a veil, or that He sendeth a messenger (angel), so that he revealeth whatsoever He wills. Verily He is Exalted, Wise” (al-Qur’an, xlii. 51) She said: He who presumes that the Messenger of Allah (may peace be upon him) concealed anything from the Book, of Allah fabricates the greatest lie against Allah. Allah says: “O Messenger! Deliver that which has been revealed to thee from thy Lord, and if thou do (it) not, thou hast not delivered His message” (al-Qur’an, v. 67). She said: He who presumes that he would inform about what was going to happen tomorrow fabricates the greatest lie against Allah. And Allah says: “Say thou (Muhammad): None in the heavens and the earth knoweth the unseen save Allah” (al-Qur’an, xxvii. 65). (Sahih Muslim, Book 001, Number 0337)
Critic:Can a believer see Allah in this life whether in a vision and/or in a visible form?
Reply:
‘No vision can grasp Him.’ (6.103)
Critic:Or can a person only know Allah from revelation, through a messenger, and/or behind a veil where Allah remains hidden?
Response:
A person can know Allah by heart,by finding His signs in the Universe.
But Allah will speak by revelation,or by a messenger or behind a veil.
And it is not for any human being that Allah should speak to him except by revelation or from behind a partition or that He sends a messenger to reveal, by His permission, what He wills. Indeed, He is Most High and Wise..42;51
Critic:If Allah can be seen, then was Aisha wrong for quoting Suras 6:103 and 42:51 to prove the contrary?
Reply:
Allah can be seen,and would be seen in Hereafter according to Quran and only wrongdoers would be veiled.Ayesha r.a. was not wrong but we have to look authentic collections only.
Nay! Surely, they (evil-doers) will be veiled from seeing their Lord that Day…..mutaffífeen 15

Clarifying the Debate on the First Encounter with the Divine:

Question: "Who was the first to witness a divine presence? Did Muhammad witness Allah or Gabriel?"

Response: According to Surah Al-Najm (53:1-18), Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) witnessed one of the greatest signs from Allah, which was in fact the angel Gabriel. The Quran informs us that Allah cannot be seen with the eyes, a concept supported by authenticated hadiths.

Question: "Who holds the correct view about Muhammad's vision? Was it Ibn Abbas and the majority, or was it Aisha, Ibn Masud, Abu Hurayra, and Abu Dharr who affirmed that Muhammad did not see his Lord, but rather Gabriel?"

Response: Authenticated hadiths from Sahih Bukhari and Sahih Muslim have significant authority. 'Aisha (may Allah be pleased with her), the Prophet's wife, categorically stated that anyone who claims the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) saw his Lord with his physical eyes attributes what is the greatest falsehood. She referenced Quranic verses emphasizing that Allah is beyond the capacity of human sight and speaks to humanity by revelation, from behind a veil, or through a messenger. The aforementioned verses are Quran 81:23, Quran 53:13, and Quran 6:103, respectively.

Question: "Is it possible for a believer to see Allah in this life, in a vision or visible form?"

Response: The Quran clearly states, "No vision can grasp Him" (Quran 6:103), indicating that Allah's essence is beyond visual perception in this life.

Question: "Can a person only truly know Allah through revelation, the messages of a messenger, and/or from behind a veil, keeping Allah unseen?"

Response: While one can perceive Allah through observing signs in the universe and in the heart's recognition, Allah communicates with humanity only through revelation, by sending a messenger, or from behind a veil, as dictated in Quran 42:51.

Question: "If Allah can be observed, was Aisha incorrect in invoking Surahs 6:103 and 42:51 to assert the opposite?"

Response: While Aisha was not incorrect and her opinion is supported by authenticated hadiths, Islamic teachings also hold that Allah will be seen by believers in the Hereafter, and it is only those who are wrongdoers who will be unable to see Him, as indicated in Surah Al-Mutaffifin (83:15). For authoritative understanding, one should refer to authentic collections of Islamic scripture.
reword this:  Who Was the First Muslim? Muhammad [6:14, 163], Moses [7:143], some Egyptians [26:51], Abraham [2:127-133, 3:67] or Adam, the first man who also received inspiration from Allah [2:37]?

Who can be designated as the foremost Muslim? Was it Muhammad, who himself made the proclamation [Quran 6:14, 163]? Could it have been Moses, as recollected in his own words [Quran 7:143], or was it certain Egyptians who expressed their submission to Allah [Quran 26:51]? Perhaps Abraham, whose submission and dedication to Allah are well-documented [Quran 2:127-133, 3:67]? Or do we look even further back to Adam, the original human, who also received guidance from Allah [Quran 2:37]?

reword this:  Allah, Adam, and the Angels. There are a great number of problems and
inconsistencies between the several accounts of Adam’s creation, Allah’s command
to prostrate before Adam, Satans refusal,

The story involving Allah, Adam, and the angels presents numerous challenges and apparent contradictions within the different versions of Adam's creation narrative, the directive from Allah to bow before Adam, and Satan's rebellion.

reformat this: 

Critic:
The conclusions outlined above lead to several contradictions since the next verses claim that no warner was sent to the Arabs, and no book was given to them, until Muhammad’s time:
Nor wast thou at the side of (the Mountain of) Tur when we called (to Moses). Yet (art thou sent) as Mercy from thy Lord, to give warning to a people to whom no warner had come before thee: in order that they may receive admonition. S. 28:46
Or do they say, “He has forged it”? Nay, it is the Truth from thy Lord, that thou mayest admonish a people to whom no warner has come before thee: in order that they may receive guidance. S. 32:3
They swore their strongest oaths by Allah that if a warner came to them, they would follow his guidance better than any (other) of the Peoples: But when a warner came to them, it has only increased their flight (from righteousness),- S. 35:42
By the Wise Koran, thou art truly among the Envoys on a straight path; the sending down of the All-mighty, the All-wise, that thou mayest warn a people WHOSE FATHERS WERE NEVER WARNED, so they are heedless. S. 36:2-6
And this is a Book which We have revealed as a blessing: so follow it and be righteous, that ye may receive mercy: Lest ye should say: “The Book was sent down to two Peoples before us, and for our part, we remained unacquainted with all that they learned by assiduous study:” Or lest ye should say: “If the Book had only been sent down to us, we should have followed its guidance better than they.” Now then hath come unto you a clear (sign) from your Lord,- and a guide and a mercy: then who could do more wrong than one who rejecteth Allah’s signs, and turneth away therefrom? In good time shall We requite those who turn away from Our signs, with a dreadful penalty, for their turning away. S. 6:155-157
But We had not given them Books which they could study, nor sent messengers to them before thee as Warners. S. 34:44
What! have We given them a Book before this, to which they are holding fast? S. 43:21
 
Reply:
Prophets Ismail a.s and Ibrahim a.s were indeed messengers,were prophets of Allah,but the verses are addressed to the Arabs of 600 AD.

Question from Critic:
The following statements suggest contradictions, as subsequent verses indicate that before Muhammad's time, the Arabs did not receive any prophet or scripture: 

S. 28:46 highlights that no previous warner had come to the people Muhammad was sent to, signifying the absence of guidance prior to his arrival. 
S. 32:3 reiterates that Muhammad's message is meant to admonish a people who hadn't received a warner beforehand. 
S. 35:42 remarks on how the people swore they would heed a warner if one was provided, yet when given one, they strayed further. 
S. 36:2-6 describes Muhammad as being tasked to warn a people whose forefathers were not warned, suggesting they lack prior knowledge of such messages. 
S. 6:155-157 speaks of the Quran as a guide and mercy, revealing the people’s lack of earlier divine books and their unfamiliarity with the teachings granted to others. 
S. 34:44 and S. 43:21 express there were no previous books acknowledged or messengers known to these populations before Muhammad.

Response:
While it is true that Prophets Ishmael and Abraham were indeed messengers and Prophets of Allah, the verses in question specifically refer to the Arab population around 600 AD, who had not encountered a warner before the arrival of Prophet Muhammad.
